content:
5 min read
Just now

--

“If JavaScript is the language your browser understands, then TypeScript is the language that helps you write better JavaScript.”
Most developers know that TypeScript adds types to JavaScript. But very few understand what actually happens after you save a .ts file.
Questions like these often come up:

• Where do the types go?

• Why can’t browsers run TypeScript directly?

• What exactly is tsc?

• Is TypeScript interpreted or compiled?

• Why does VS Code know my variable names before I even run the program?
In this article, we’ll answer all of these questions by looking inside the TypeScript compiler.

Why Do We Need a Compiler?
Imagine you write the following TypeScript code:
function greet(name: string): string {
return `Hello, ${name}`;
}console.log(greet("Himanshu"));
The browser understands JavaScript, not TypeScript.
If you tried to execute the file directly, the browser would encounter syntax it doesn’t recognize, such as : string.
That means your TypeScript code needs to be transformed into JavaScript first.
This transformation is handled by the TypeScript Compiler, commonly referred to as tsc.

What Is tsc?
tsc is the official TypeScript compiler.
It performs two major jobs:

• It checks your code for errors before execution.

• It converts TypeScript into plain JavaScript.
Think of it as a translator.
TypeScript
│
▼
+-------------------+
| TypeScript |
| Compiler |
| (tsc) |
+-------------------+
│
▼
JavaScript
│
▼
Browser / Node.jsWithout this compilation step, your TypeScript code cannot run in JavaScript environments.

Installing TypeScript
Before exploring the compiler, install TypeScript globally:
npm install -g typescriptOr install it in a project:
npm install --save-dev typescriptVerify the installation:
tsc --versionYou should see the installed version printed in your terminal.

Your First Compilation
Create a file named app.ts:
const message: string = "Welcome to TypeScript";console.log(message);
Compile it:
tsc app.tsA new file appears:
app.jsOpen it:
const message = "Welcome to TypeScript";console.log(message);
Notice something important?
The type annotation (: string) is gone.
The browser doesn’t need it.

Type Erasure
One of the most important concepts in TypeScript is type erasure.
Type information exists only while the compiler analyzes your code.
Once compilation finishes, all type annotations are removed.
Example:
let age: number = 30;Compiled JavaScript:
let age = 30;This means types help developers, editors, and build tools — but they don’t exist at runtime.

The Five Stages of the TypeScript Compiler
Understanding the compiler pipeline makes many TypeScript features easier to understand.
Write Code
│
▼
Parser
│
▼
Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
│
▼
Binder
│
▼
Type Checker
│
▼
Emitter
│
▼
JavaScript OutputLet’s examine each stage.
1. Parser
The parser reads your TypeScript source code and verifies that the syntax is valid.
Example:
const name = "Himanshu";The parser identifies:

• const keyword

• variable name

• assignment operator

• string literal
If you accidentally write:
const = "Hello";The parser immediately reports a syntax error because a variable name is missing.
At this stage, the compiler only checks whether the code follows the language grammar. It doesn’t yet understand types.

2. Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
After parsing, the compiler converts your code into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
Think of the AST as a structured representation of your program.
For example, the statement:
const age = 25;becomes something conceptually like:
VariableDeclaration
├── Keyword: const
├── Identifier: age
└── Initializer
└── NumericLiteral: 25The compiler uses this tree for all later analysis instead of working directly with raw text.
This tree is also what powers many editor features, code formatters, and linters.

3. Binder
The binder connects declarations with symbols.
Imagine this code:
const age = 25;console.log(age);
The binder records that age refers to the same variable in both places.
Without binding, the compiler wouldn’t know which declaration a reference belongs to.

4. Type Checker
This is where TypeScript becomes powerful.
The type checker analyzes your program to ensure that values are used correctly.
Example:
let age: number = 25;age = "Twenty Five";
The parser is happy because the syntax is valid.
The type checker is not.
It reports:
Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'Notice the distinction:

• Syntax is correct.

• Types are incorrect.
This separation is one of the reasons TypeScript can provide such precise feedback.

5. Emitter
Finally, the emitter generates JavaScript.
Input:
const age: number = 25;Output:
const age = 25;The emitted JavaScript is what actually runs in the browser or in Node.js.

Compilation vs. Transpilation
You may hear both terms used when discussing TypeScript.

• Compilation generally means converting source code into another form, often with analysis and optimization.

• Transpilation means converting one high-level language into another high-level language.
TypeScript performs both:

• It compiles your code by analyzing syntax and types.

• It transpiles your TypeScript into JavaScript.
Understanding this distinction helps when discussing build tools like Babel, SWC, or esbuild.

Real-World Example
Imagine you’re building a React application.
You define a component like this:
type ButtonProps = {
label: string;
};function Button({ label }: ButtonProps) {
return <button>{label}</button>;
}
Later, another developer uses it incorrectly:
<Button label={42} />The type checker immediately reports an error before the application is even launched.
Without TypeScript, this mistake might only surface during manual testing or after deployment.

Common Misconceptions
“TypeScript makes my application faster.”
Not directly.
TypeScript improves development by catching mistakes early. The generated JavaScript typically performs similarly to handwritten JavaScript because type information is removed during compilation.
“TypeScript runs in the browser.”
It doesn’t.
Browsers execute JavaScript, not TypeScript.
“TypeScript changes JavaScript behavior.”
Generally, no. Its primary role is to analyze your code and emit equivalent JavaScript. Certain language features may require helper code depending on your target environment, but the goal is to preserve behavior.

Interview Questions
1. What is the role of tsc?
It analyzes TypeScript code, reports type errors, and emits JavaScript.
2. What is an AST?
An Abstract Syntax Tree is a tree representation of the structure of source code, used internally by the compiler.
3. What is type erasure?
The removal of type annotations during compilation so the output is valid JavaScript.
4. Does the parser check types?
No. The parser validates syntax. Type checking happens in a later stage.
5. Why can VS Code provide autocomplete before running the code?
Because the TypeScript language service builds on the same parsing, binding, and type-checking infrastructure used by the compiler.

Practice Exercise
Create a file named math.ts:
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
return a + b;
}console.log(add(10, 20));
console.log(add("10", 20));

• Compile the file with tsc.

• Observe the type error for the second call.

• Inspect the generated JavaScript.

• Notice that the type annotations have been removed.
Ask yourself:

• At which compiler stage was the syntax validated?

• At which stage was the type mismatch detected?

• What code did the emitter generate?
Understanding these questions reinforces the compiler pipeline.

Key Takeaways

• TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript that requires compilation.

• The tsc compiler parses, binds, checks types, and emits JavaScript.

• Types exist only during development and compilation.

• Browsers execute JavaScript, not TypeScript.

• The compiler pipeline explains why TypeScript can provide rich editor tooling and early error detection.

Coming Next
In the next article, we’ll move from how the compiler works to the TypeScript type system itself. We’ll answer foundational questions such as:

• What is a type?

• How does TypeScript infer types?

• What are primitive, literal, union, and intersection types?

• What is type widening and narrowing?

• How does control-flow analysis make TypeScript smarter than many developers realize?
By the end of that article, you’ll understand not just how to write types, but how the compiler reasons about them.

Thanks!
